text: Designing a good REST API makes your service easier for developers to use. First, choose clear, noun-based endpoints like `/users` or `/orders` rather than verbs. Always use appropriate HTTP status codes: 200 for success, 201 for created resources, 400 for bad requests, and 404 for not found. Error responses should include helpful messages. Documentation is essential—tools like Swagger or OpenAPI allow developers to understand your API quickly. Never forget about versioning; include the version in the URL, for example `/v1/users`, so changes don't break existing clients. Following these practices leads to reliable, maintainable, and user-friendly APIs.
